rows.v: The opening in a lens that controls how much light enters, measured in f-stops. It also governs depth of field — how much of the image is in focus.
She opened the aperture to f/1.8 to blur the background and let in more light.
From Latin 'apertura' (an opening). Confusingly, smaller f-numbers mean a wider opening — f/1.8 is wide open, f/16 is a tiny pinhole.
How long the camera's shutter stays open to expose the sensor, measured in fractions of a second. It controls motion — freezing or blurring it.
A fast shutter speed of 1/2000 froze the splashing water in midair.
Descriptive of how quickly the shutter opens and closes. Fast speeds freeze action; slow speeds blur it, capturing motion as streaks of light.
The sensor's sensitivity to light. Low ISO gives clean images in bright light; high ISO brightens dark scenes but adds graininess ('noise').
He cranked the ISO to 6400 to shoot the dim concert, accepting some grain for a usable shot.
Named for the International Organization for Standardization, which standardized film sensitivity ratings — a system carried over to digital sensors.
The overall brightness of an image, determined by the balance of aperture, shutter speed, and ISO — the 'exposure triangle.' Too much is overexposed; too little, under.
She nailed the exposure, capturing detail in both the bright sky and the shadowed rocks.
From 'expose' — the sensor or film is 'exposed' to light. The three controls trade off against each other, the fundamental balancing act of photography.
A unit of light measurement. Each 'stop' doubles or halves the light — a key concept tying together aperture, shutter, and ISO adjustments.
He brightened the shot by one stop, doubling the light hitting the sensor.
From the historical lens 'stops' (physical plates with holes). One stop is a doubling or halving of light — the universal currency of exposure adjustments.
'Overexposed' images are too bright, losing detail in the highlights; 'underexposed' images are too dark, losing detail in the shadows. Both lose information.
The snowy scene came out overexposed, the bright drifts washed to featureless white.
Descriptive: 'over' or 'under' the ideal amount of exposure. Lost highlight or shadow detail often can't be recovered, making correct exposure critical.
How the camera measures the light in a scene to suggest an exposure. Different metering modes weigh the frame differently — the whole scene, the center, or a spot.
She switched to spot metering to expose correctly for the backlit subject's face.
From 'meter' (to measure). The light meter reads the scene's brightness; the mode determines which parts of the frame it prioritizes.
A graph showing the distribution of brightness in an image, from dark (left) to light (right). A precise tool for judging exposure beyond the screen.
The histogram bunched against the right edge, warning him the highlights were clipping.
From Greek 'histos' (mast, web) + 'gramma' (drawing) — a statistical bar chart adapted to show how an image's tones are distributed.
The lens measurement (in millimeters) that determines magnification and angle of view. Short focal lengths see wide; long ones zoom in tight.
He switched to a 200mm focal length to fill the frame with the distant bird.
Technically the distance from the lens's optical center to the sensor when focused at infinity. In practice, it's the number that tells you how 'zoomed in' a lens is.
How much of the image, from front to back, appears in sharp focus. 'Shallow' depth blurs all but the subject; 'deep' depth keeps everything sharp.
A shallow depth of field threw the busy background into a creamy blur, isolating the portrait.
Descriptive: the 'depth' of the zone that's in 'field' (focus). Controlled mainly by aperture, it's a primary creative tool for directing the viewer's eye.
The aesthetic quality of the out-of-focus areas in an image, especially blurred points of light rendered as soft orbs. Prized for its dreamy look.
The fairy lights melted into beautiful round bokeh behind the subject.
From Japanese 'boke' (blur, haze). It refers not to the amount of blur but its pleasing quality — the character of how a lens renders what's out of focus.
A 'prime' lens has one fixed focal length; a 'zoom' lens covers a range. Primes are sharper and faster; zooms are more versatile.
She loved her prime for its razor sharpness but packed a zoom for travel flexibility.
'Prime' from the sense of fixed or fundamental; 'zoom' from the variable lens that lets you zoom in and out. A classic tradeoff of quality versus convenience.
A 'wide-angle' lens captures a broad view, exaggerating space; a 'telephoto' lens magnifies distant subjects, compressing space. Opposite ends of the lens spectrum.
He shot the sweeping landscape wide, then switched to telephoto to isolate a distant peak.
'Wide' for the broad angle of view; 'telephoto' from Greek 'tele' (far) + 'photo' (light) — a lens that photographs faraway things up close.
Extreme close-up photography that captures tiny subjects at life-size or greater magnification, revealing detail invisible to the naked eye.
Her macro shot of the dewdrop revealed a tiny world reflected inside it.
From Greek 'makros' (large) — paradoxically, it makes small things appear large, magnifying insects, flowers, and textures to dramatic scale.
The adjustment that makes the subject appear sharp and clear. A photo can be in focus, out of focus, or selectively focused on one element.
He locked focus on the runner's eyes, letting the rest of the frame fall soft.
From Latin 'focus' (hearth, fireplace) — later adopted in optics for the point where light rays converge to form a sharp image.
The setting that keeps colors accurate under different light sources, so white looks white whether under sunlight, shade, or indoor bulbs.
She corrected the white balance to remove the orange cast from the tungsten lamps.
Descriptive: it 'balances' the image so whites render neutrally. Without it, photos pick up color casts — bluish in shade, orange under household bulbs.
The period shortly after sunrise and before sunset when light is soft, warm, and golden. Photographers' favorite natural light.
They scheduled the shoot for golden hour, when the low sun bathed everything in warm light.
Named for the warm, golden quality of low-angle sunlight. The sun's low position softens shadows and casts a flattering glow prized for portraits and landscapes.
'Hard light' is direct and creates sharp, dark shadows and high contrast; 'soft light' is diffused, gentle, and creates gradual shadows. Two fundamental light qualities.
He diffused the harsh flash into soft light to flatter the subject's skin.
Descriptive of how light renders shadows. A small or distant source (like the bare sun) is 'hard'; a large or diffused one (like an overcast sky) is 'soft'.
Light coming from behind the subject, toward the camera. It can create silhouettes, glowing rim-light, or dramatic haze.
The backlight rimmed her hair with a glowing halo against the dark background.
Descriptive: the light is 'behind' the subject. Tricky to expose for, but it produces some of photography's most striking, luminous effects.
A secondary light (or reflector) used to brighten shadows created by the main light, reducing harsh contrast. It 'fills in' the dark areas.
A reflector bounced fill light into the shadows, softening the contrast on his face.
Descriptive: it 'fills' the shadows with light. A core tool of lighting setups, balancing the brightness between the lit and shadowed sides of a subject.
Color Temperature — a measure (in Kelvin) of how warm (orange) or cool (blue) a light source is. Candlelight is warm; midday sky is cool.
The warm 3000K bulbs gave the room a cozy glow, far warmer than cool daylight.
Borrowed from physics, where heating an object shifts its glow from red to blue. Counterintuitively, lower Kelvin numbers look warmer, higher numbers cooler.
The range of brightness a camera can capture, from the darkest shadow to the brightest highlight, with detail retained. Wider range handles high-contrast scenes better.
The camera's wide dynamic range held detail in both the bright window and the dim room.
From 'dynamic' (range of variation) — the span between the faintest and strongest signal the sensor can record before losing detail to pure black or white.
A guideline that divides the frame into a 3×3 grid; placing key elements along the lines or intersections creates more balanced, dynamic images.
She placed the horizon on the lower third line rather than dead center for a more pleasing balance.
A simplification of older artistic ideas about balanced composition. The off-center placement tends to feel more natural and engaging than centering everything.
Lines within an image (roads, fences, rivers) that guide the viewer's eye toward the subject or through the scene. A powerful compositional tool.
The winding road served as a leading line, drawing the eye toward the mountains beyond.
Descriptive: the lines 'lead' the viewer's gaze. Photographers hunt for natural lines to create depth and direct attention exactly where they want it.
Using elements in the scene (a doorway, branches, an arch) to surround and draw attention to the subject. A frame within the frame.
He used the stone archway for framing, the subject perfectly enclosed within it.
From the literal 'frame' — a foreground element that encloses the subject, adding depth, context, and focus, like a built-in picture frame.
The empty or uncluttered area around a subject. Generous negative space can make a subject stand out and give an image room to breathe.
Vast negative space of empty sky left the lone tree small but powerfully isolated.
From art theory, where 'negative space' is the area around and between subjects. Used deliberately, emptiness becomes a powerful compositional element.
A balanced composition where elements mirror each other across an axis. Symmetry creates a sense of order, harmony, and sometimes striking formality.
The reflection in the still lake created perfect symmetry, top and bottom mirroring exactly.
From Greek 'symmetria' (agreement in measure). Our eyes are drawn to symmetry; photographers use it for impact, or break it deliberately for tension.
'Foreground' is what's nearest the camera; 'background' is what's farthest. Layering them creates depth in a two-dimensional image.
He placed flowers in the foreground and mountains in the background to build a sense of depth.
Descriptive: the 'fore' (front) and 'back' grounds of the scene. Using both gives a flat photograph the illusion of three-dimensional depth.
Vantage Point — the position and angle from which a photo is taken. Shooting from high, low, or unexpected angles transforms an ordinary subject.
By crouching for a low vantage point, she made the small dog look heroic against the sky.
From Old French 'avantage' (advantage) — a 'vantage' point offers an advantageous view. Changing it is the simplest way to make a tired subject fresh.
'RAW' files capture all the sensor's data for maximum editing flexibility; 'JPEG' files are compressed and processed, smaller but less editable. A core workflow choice.
She shot in RAW to have full latitude to recover highlights and adjust color later.
'RAW' (not an acronym, just the word) signals the unprocessed sensor data; JPEG is named for the Joint Photographic Experts Group that created the format.
Obsessively examining an image at extreme magnification to scrutinize fine detail and sharpness. Often a sign of fussing over gear instead of making pictures.
He spent the evening pixel-peeping, zooming to 200% to compare lens sharpness corner to corner.
A faintly mocking term for inspecting individual pixels — gear enthusiasts obsessing over technical minutiae that viewers would never notice.
Repeatedly checking the camera's rear screen after each shot, often with delight. The habit of reviewing instead of staying ready for the next moment.
Stop chimping and keep shooting — you'll miss the action staring at your last frame.
Said to come from the 'ooh-ooh' noises photographers make admiring their shots, likened to chimpanzees — a gently teasing bit of photographer slang.
Affectionate nickname for a 50mm prime lens, beloved for being cheap, sharp, fast, and versatile. Often a photographer's first 'real' lens upgrade.
Every beginner should grab a nifty fifty — it's the best value lens you can buy.
Rhyming slang celebrating the 50mm lens's 'nifty' combination of low price and high quality, a near-universal recommendation for new photographers.
Describes highlights so overexposed they've lost all detail, turning pure featureless white. Unrecoverable information, a common exposure error.
The bright sky was completely blown out, just a sheet of white where clouds should be.
From the sense of detail being 'blown out' or obliterated by excess light — the brightest areas clip to pure white with nothing left to recover.
Describes an image in perfect, crisp focus — as sharp as a tack. The highest praise for image sharpness.
Shot on a tripod at the right settings, the landscape came out tack-sharp from edge to edge.
From the sharpness of a tack's point — vivid slang for an image so crisply focused that fine details are rendered with perfect clarity.
